title: Dog Rigid Limbs and Arched Back: Is It an Emergency?
meta_desc: If your dog has rigid limbs and an arched back, it may be experiencing opisthotonus or tetany. This is a high-priority emergency. Seek care now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Opisthotonus, characterized by rigid limbs and an arched back, is a critical medical emergency requiring immediate veterinary attention. Capturing a short video of the episode can help your veterinarian diagnose the cause more quickly.

What is opisthotonus and why is my dog’s body rigid?

When a dog experiences rigid limbs and a severely arched back, it is often a clinical sign known as opisthotonus. This physical state occurs when the muscles along the neck and spine contract uncontrollably, causing the head to pull backward. This can be caused by neurological trauma, severe poisoning (such as snail bait or strychnine), tetanus, or inflammatory brain diseases. It is a sign that the central nervous system is under significant distress.

Is my dog’s muscle rigidity and arched back a medical emergency?

  • Yes. This condition is a critical medical emergency with a "High" urgency level.
  • Rigid posture and muscle spasms can interfere with your dog's ability to breathe.
  • The condition can lead to a dangerously high body temperature (hyperthermia).
  • Contact the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.
  • Prepare to transport your dog safely to the hospital.

How does a video or photo of my dog’s symptoms help the veterinarian?

  • Capturing a brief video allows the veterinary team to see the exact nature of the muscle contractions.
  • It helps the doctor assess the dog's level of consciousness during the episode.
  • Visual evidence helps differentiate between seizures, vestibular issues, or toxin exposure before the physical exam begins.
  • Providing this information can save life-saving minutes during the intake process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Stiffness and rigidity in a limb, progressing to other limbs, may indicate localized tetanus, which can advance anteriorly. Generalized tetanus can cause spasms of the neck and back muscles, leading to extension of the head and neck (opisthotonos), and stiffness of the leg muscles, resulting in a "sawhorse" stance. Severe cases of tetanus may lead to recumbency with opisthotonos and reflex muscle spasms. Strychnine poisoning can also induce a "sawhorse" stance due to extreme extensor rigidity, accompanied by hyperthermia, tetanic seizures, and potential respiratory arrest. Fibrocartilagenous embolism causing spinal cord infarction can also lead to rigid limbs. Given the potential for life-threatening complications like respiratory paralysis and anoxia, these signs warrant immediate veterinary attention.

Chapter: Neurology, Infectious Diseases

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 612)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog any over-the-counter medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications or sedatives to a dog in this state, as it may worsen the underlying cause or interfere with emergency treatments.

faqs.question: How should I transport my dog to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place your dog on a flat, sturdy surface like a piece of plywood or a thick blanket to keep them stable. Keep the car quiet and dark to minimize stimulation to the nervous system.

faqs.question: Could this be a seizure?
faqs.answer: Yes, rigid limbs (tonic phase) are common in seizures, but regardless of the cause, any episode involving loss of motor control and rigid posture requires immediate veterinary evaluation.
